evals.prompt: Design a 6-week JTBD discovery sprint for an enterprise client modernizing wealth-management onboarding.
evals.expected_output: Should produce a 6-week sprint plan with named stages: Week 1 — hypothesis design (suspected jobs across 2-3 archetypes: HNW individual, mass-affluent, advisor-led), discovery brief, interview guide drafted, recruiting kicked off (target 12-16 to net 8-10 useful per archetype). Week 2 — interview round 1 (8-12 customers) using switch-interview structure (Klement/Moesta), focus on last-time-they-onboarded stories, four-forces capture (push/pull/anxiety/habit). Week 2-3 — synthesis (affinity mapping, first-pass job stories, switch trigger taxonomy). Week 3 — round 2 interviews (6-10) testing job stories. Week 4 — circumstance mapping + competing solutions inventory (existing onboarding, white-glove advisor, doing-nothing/staying-with-current-firm). Week 4-5 — opportunity prioritization (scorecard: customer value × competitive gap × feasibility). Week 5-6 — synthesis report + Gate 1 brief with riskiest assumption identified. Should specify deliverables (job stories, force map, opportunity scorecard, Gate 1 brief) and required talent (lead interviewer with JTBD chops, ethnographer for observation, synthesis facilitator).

evals.prompt: Coach my team through their first switch interview. We have 14 customers lined up across 3 segments who recently switched financial-planning tools.
evals.expected_output: Should provide structured coaching: (1) Recruiting note — 14 to net ~10 useful is right ratio; mix of switched-recently (last 6-12 months for recall) and considered-but-didn't-switch (status-quo'd) reveals anxiety patterns; (2) Interview structure (90 min) with the 6 phases: First Thought (10 min — when they realized something needed to change), Passive Looking (15 — what they noticed but didn't act on, and for how long), Active Looking (20 — what triggered the shift, what they considered), Decision (25 — what tipped them, the four forces), First Use (15 — moment of progress), Ongoing Use (10 — what's different); (3) Key coaching moves — stay in past tense, ask about specific recent experiences not hypotheticals, don't pitch the product, don't suggest solutions, dig into 'why' 3-4 layers, capture customer's actual language; (4) Anti-patterns to flag — 'would you pay for X' (biased), suggesting solutions mid-interview, aggregating across segments too early; (5) Synthesis prep — after each interview, plot four forces; after all 10, run affinity mapping. Recommend a practice run with a peer before the first real interview.

evals.prompt: Synthesize these 12 interview transcripts into validated job stories and an opportunity scorecard. [Hypothetical transcripts about consumer banking customers' moments of frustration with their current bank.]
evals.expected_output: Should produce 5 deliverables: (1) Job stories — 3-5 distinct, in 'When [circumstance], I want [motivation], so I can [outcome]' format, supported by quote evidence from transcripts; (2) Force map — push of situation (current frustrations), pull of new (aspirations), anxiety of switching (fears), habit of present (inertia) per archetype; (3) Trigger event taxonomy — what specifically caused customers to start looking; (4) Competing solutions inventory — what they considered (other banks, fintech apps, doing-nothing, mortgaging-with-broker, etc.) and how they ranked; (5) Unmet needs ranked by customer value × competitive gap × emotional weight. Should resist over-aggregation — preserve circumstance distinctions across segments. Should call out language patterns (verbatim phrases) for use in messaging. Should flag the riskiest assumption to test next, in service of Gate 1 brief. If the transcripts are thin or skewed, surface that as data limitation rather than producing forced synthesis.

evals.prompt: Our discovery is producing CX insights, not JTBD. How do I reset the team's frame?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the difference: CX insights live in current-state journeys (touchpoint pain, friction, satisfaction); JTBD lives in pre-product circumstance (the job customers were trying to do that brought them to any solution at all). Common pattern: teams default to journey mapping their existing product instead of researching the job that drove customers to want any product. Reset moves: (1) Ban product-name in interviews — customers can't reference 'when I use [our product]' for the first 60 minutes; (2) Switch the interview anchor from 'how do you use this' to 'what were you trying to do that brought you to consider any solution' (3) Refocus on switch interviews specifically — last-time-they-switched stories produce JTBD; satisfaction surveys produce CX; (4) Cross-link with cx-jtbd skill (sister plugin) for parallel CX work that can run in tandem; (5) Consider the team may need different interviewer talent — JTBD work is harder than journey mapping and often requires re-staffing. Note: both have a place. CX research informs current-product improvement (H1 work). JTBD informs new-bet opportunity (H2/H3 work). Don't substitute one for the other.

evals.prompt: We have an idea — connected fitness for older adults. Design discovery to ground it.
evals.expected_output: Should refuse to research the idea directly. Reframe: research the *job* customers (older adults, ~65+) are doing in fitness/health/social-connection, BEFORE assuming connected fitness is the right solution. Structure: (1) Hypothesis — what jobs might older adults be hiring fitness products to do? (functional: stay capable; emotional: feel autonomous, not old; social: connection, not isolation); (2) Recruiting — 12-16 across age bands (65-72, 72-80, 80+) and circumstances (active, recovering, sedentary); (3) Switch interviews about the last time they engaged with fitness/health/social connection — what triggered, what they tried, what stuck; (4) Observation — shadow customers in their environment (home, gym, community center, telehealth visits); (5) Competing solutions — what they currently do (walking, senior centers, YMCA, telehealth, family encouragement, doing nothing); (6) Synthesis — most likely outcome is the real opportunity is NOT 'connected fitness' but something adjacent (e.g., medical-supervised social fitness, family-engaged movement, AI-companion for daily activity). Reframe before solution. Output: discovery report with recommendation to pivot or persevere on the original 'connected fitness' frame.

evals.prompt: Design observation discipline for a discovery sprint. Most of my team wants to skip it and just do interviews.
evals.expected_output: Should make the case for observation explicitly: interviews capture what customers say; observation captures what they do — and these often diverge. Reserve 30-40% of discovery effort for observation. Structure: (1) Contextual inquiry — watch the customer do the thing in their actual environment (home, office, store); ask 'why' at moments of friction; (2) Workaround spotting — workarounds are gold; they reveal jobs people are doing without a real product (e.g., spreadsheet tracking when no tool exists); (3) Spreadsheet hunting — if customers run their work in Excel/email, that's an unmet job; (4) Off-stage moments — what happens before/after the obvious touchpoint; the real job often lives there. Practical recommendations: (a) shadow each interviewee for 60-90 min in addition to the 90-min interview, when feasible; (b) ride-alongs, in-home visits, or screen-shares depending on context; (c) capture observation field notes separately from interview notes; (d) synthesize observations BEFORE interview synthesis to avoid contamination by interview language; (e) when full observation isn't possible, at minimum ask customers to walk through their actual environment on video. Note: teams that skip observation produce thinner JTBD insights and miss workaround-driven opportunities (often the highest-value).

evals.prompt: We've done 8 customer interviews and produced a 70-page report. The team thinks we're ready for Gate 1. Are we?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose readiness against Gate 1 criteria, not page count. Required artifacts: (1) Validated job statements — 3-5 distinct, in JTBD format, with evidence; (2) Circumstance map — when, where, why each job arises; (3) Competing solutions inventory including doing-nothing; (4) Riskiest assumption identified — the single belief the bet most depends on, with proposed test; (5) Recommended next-stage hypothesis (problem statement for Method Validator). 70 pages is concerning — usually a sign of unsynthesized data, not validated insight. Ask: (a) Are 3-5 job stories named with evidence? Or is it a transcript dump? (b) Has the team reduced 70 pages to a 1-page Gate 1 brief? If not, they haven't synthesized; (c) Did anyone outside the team review the synthesis? Peer review surfaces over-fitting; (d) Are there any invalidated hypotheses? If everything 'confirmed,' that's confirmation bias not discovery. Recommend: (1) force the team to produce a 1-page Gate 1 brief before scheduling Gate 1; (2) peer review with another innovation team or with the leadership-coach for honest critique; (3) if the riskiest assumption hasn't been named and a test designed, the team is not Gate-1 ready, regardless of page count.

evals.prompt: Coach me on the five Innovator's DNA discovery skills. Where am I weakest?
evals.expected_output: Should explain the five skills (associating, questioning, observing, networking, experimenting) and their characteristics, then ask 1-2 diagnostic questions to identify the user's weakest area: (a) When did you last spend 90 min observing a customer in their context? (Observing); (b) When did you last have a substantive conversation with someone outside your industry? (Networking); (c) When did you last build a cheap prototype to test an idea? (Experimenting); (d) When did you last force-flip an industry assumption ('what if the opposite were true?')? (Questioning); (e) When did you last connect a pattern from an unrelated field to your work? (Associating). Most professionals are weakest at Observing or Networking — both feel uncomfortable and are easy to skip. Provide a 30-day practice plan for the user's weakest area: e.g., for Observing — 4 contextual inquiries per month at 60-90 min each; for Networking — 1 cross-industry interview per month, deliberate; for Experimenting — one pretotype per quarter on a current idea. Reference Innovator's DNA twin study finding (1/3 nature, 2/3 nurture) — the skills can be built deliberately.
